questions.question_summary: Identifies which property (flow, compressibility, shape, volume) of a substance remains constant during the transition from a liquid to a gas.
questions.reasoning_focus: Recognizing macroscopic properties of gases compared to liquids.

questions.question_summary: Explains why iron is difficult to compress while oxygen is easy to compress at room temperature using particle theory.
questions.reasoning_focus: Correlating ease of compression with the distance between particles in different states of matter.

questions.question_summary: Explains the decrease in gas pressure within a syringe when the volume is increased at a constant temperature.
questions.reasoning_focus: Relating increased volume to a decrease in the frequency of particle collisions with the container walls.

questions.question_summary: Compares the separation and speed of particles in a hot gas versus a cool liquid while at the same pressure.
questions.reasoning_focus: Understanding how temperature and physical state affect particle distance and velocity.

questions.question_summary: Requires calculating the final pressure of a fixed mass of gas that expands from 1.2 cubic meters to 1.8 cubic meters at constant temperature.
questions.reasoning_focus: Applying the mathematical relationship p1V1 = p2V2 to determine pressure changes.

questions.question_summary: Explains the increase in pressure of a gas in a sealed container when heat is applied.
questions.reasoning_focus: Identifying that increased thermal energy results in greater forces exerted by particles striking container walls.
